tweets.text:
Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

With the US and Iran now trading blows, there were no US escorts in the last 24 hours. Inbound VLCCs unrelated to Iran fell to zero for the last 2 days.

The key to the oil math was always inbound tankers, not outbound tankers. With no incoming VLCCs, production shut-in will rise, which is the essence of the oil market deficit.

As you will see in the @MarineTraffic video, AIS spoofing appears about halfway through. According to @MenchOsint 's reporting today, IRGC speed boats turned vessels around in the Oman lane. This will also prevent shadow transits from taking place.

With US conducting the largest strike since the ceasefire took place in April, it will be interesting to see what develops further.

I have included my inbound tanker list below for the last 3 days.

tweets.text:
US refineries are maxed out, but we can't build product storage because 1) end-user demand remains healthy and 2) we are exporting everything we can because the rest of the world is short on products.

Meanwhile, prices have to keep going higher domestically to try and price out exports. And since the rest of the world is short, this only pulls prices higher faster.

And since China lifted the product export ban, teapot refineries can buy whatever crude they want and still make money because refining margins are so elevated.

The next big catalyst to watch is what China does with domestic fuel pricing. If it matches to global prices, watch out. Teapot refinery utilization will hit a new record high, which means the spread between crude and crack spreads will come down pushing crude meaningfully higher.
tweets.full_text:
US refineries are maxed out, but we can't build product storage because 1) end-user demand remains healthy and 2) we are exporting everything we can because the rest of the world is short on products.

Meanwhile, prices have to keep going higher domestically to try and price out exports. And since the rest of the world is short, this only pulls prices higher faster.

And since China lifted the product export ban, teapot refineries can buy whatever crude they want and still make money because refining margins are so elevated.

The next big catalyst to watch is what China does with domestic fuel pricing. If it matches to global prices, watch out. Teapot refinery utilization will hit a new record high, which means the spread between crude and crack spreads will come down pushing crude meaningfully higher.
